1.note: James commends Job's perseverance to a suffering community: 'You have heard of the steadfastness of Job, and you have seen the purpose of the Lord, how the Lord is compassionate and merciful.' Job's endurance through undeserved suffering is the paradigm of faith-in-the-dark that James holds up as the model for trials. The 'purpose of the Lord' James mentions is the vindication of Job's integrity at the end — visible only in retrospect, which is how faith must function in the midst of suffering.

6.note: The divine council scene — 'the sons of God came to present themselves before the LORD, and Satan also came among them' — reveals the cosmic-courtroom framework of OT theology: accusation happens before God. Revelation 12:10 names the dragon as 'the accuser of our brothers...who accuses them day and night before our God.' The heavenly council in Job 1-2 is the backstory that John's apocalyptic vision resolves: what the cross achieved is precisely the expulsion of the accuser from heaven.

The adversary (haSSāṭān — the Accuser) stands before YHWH to accuse Job just as he stands at the right hand of Joshua the high priest to accuse him in Zechariah 3:1. Both scenes show the Accuser's activity as cosmic complaint before the divine throne — and in both cases, YHWH rebukes him and vindicates the accused. The pattern culminates in Christ, who silences the accuser permanently by bearing the verdict himself (Rom 8:33-34).

21.note: 'Naked I came from my mother's womb, and naked shall I return. The LORD gave, and the LORD has taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD.' Paul echoes Job's detachment: 'we brought nothing into the world, and we cannot take anything out of the world' (1 Tim 6:7), grounding contentment in the same theology — what we hold is held on loan. Job's doxology amid loss is the OT's purest expression of the posture Paul commands.

Job's 'blessed be the name of the LORD' after total loss is the OT precedent for Paul's 'I have learned, in whatever situation I am, to be content' (Phil 4:11). Both express that worship is not contingent on circumstances. The difference is that Job learns it through crisis; Paul through a lifetime of imprisonment, beatings, and shipwrecks. Both reach the same theology: the LORD's name is blessable precisely when it costs most.

The LORD gave, and the LORD has taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD — Job's response to total loss; Paul's 'I have learned, in whatever state I am, to be content' echoes the Job pattern of accepting both abundance and loss from the hand of God

1.note: The second divine council meeting reprises the first — 'Satan also came among them to present himself before the LORD.' The accuser's persistence in presenting himself before God (twice now, unbidden) is the OT picture of what John sees expelled at the cross: the accuser who 'accuses them day and night before our God' is cast down by 'the blood of the Lamb and by the word of their testimony' (Rev 12:11). The patient, repeated accusation of Job prefigures the permanent accusation that Christ's death finally answers.

10.note: Job refuses to sin against God even when his wife tells him to curse God and die: 'Shall we receive good from God, and shall we not receive evil?' The author of Hebrews frames suffering similarly: 'It is for discipline that you have to endure. God is treating you as sons' (Heb 12:7). Job doesn't have this explanation — he suffers without knowing why — yet his question implicitly grants God sovereignty over both good and evil in a way that Hebrews' theology of discipline presupposes.

Job receives evil from God without sinning — his refusal to curse is the act of faith that his wife cannot comprehend. James calls the community to the same disposition: 'Count it all joy, my brothers, when you meet trials of various kinds, for you know that the testing of your faith produces steadfastness' (Jas 1:2-3). Job is the OT demonstration that faith-tested-and-held is possible; James's exhortation presupposes that it is also commanded.

3.note: Job's desperate lament ('Let the day perish on which I was born') expresses suffering that finds no human parallel in the OT. The depth of lament — wishing for non-existence rather than continued anguish — is the OT's closest approach to Jesus in Gethsemane: 'My soul is very sorrowful, even to death' (Matt 26:38). Both are fully innocent sufferers under crushing weight; but where Job laments the day of his birth, Jesus accepts the cup and goes to the cross. The distance between them is the distance between the type and the one who exhausts suffering's deepest logic on behalf of others.

'Let the day perish on which I was born' — Job's lament is echoed almost verbatim by Jeremiah (Jer 20:14-18), creating an OT tradition of prophetic-lament that cries out from the depths of innocent suffering. Both Job and Jeremiah are figures of faithful suffering who did not get immediate answers — anticipating the one who cried 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' (Ps 22:1; Matt 27:46) while remaining obedient to death.

17.note: Eliphaz asks: 'Can mortal man be in the right before God? Can a man be pure before his Maker?' His question assumes the answer is 'no' — and intends to convict Job of secret sin. Paul gives the same answer but for different purposes: 'by works of the law no human being will be justified in his sight' (Rom 3:20). Eliphaz uses the truth to condemn Job; Paul uses it to drive all to the righteousness that comes through faith in Christ.

13.note: He catches the wise in their craftiness

7.note: “People are born to trouble as naturally as sparks fly upward” — Eliphaz’s observation about the universality of human suffering resonates with Paul’s theological account in Romans 5:12: “sin entered the world through one man, and death through sin, and in this way death spread to all people”; Eliphaz intends the maxim as a rebuke to Job (suffering is natural, not exceptional), but the deeper truth he accidentally voices is that human life under the fall is structurally oriented toward affliction — the very condition that Christ assumes and transforms in the incarnation

9.note: “Who does great and unsearchable things, marvellous things beyond counting” — Eliphaz’s doxology of divine inscrutability is the OT cognate of Paul’s climactic exclamation in Romans 11:33: “Oh, the depth of the riches of the wisdom and knowledge of God! How unsearchable his judgments, and his ways beyond tracing out!”; both texts invoke the category of divine “unsearchableness” (ḥēqer; Gk. anexeraunētos) as the proper response to encountering the limits of human wisdom about God’s ways — a category that Job will himself deploy against his friends

11.note: “Who lifts up the lowly on high, and raises those in mourning to safety” — Eliphaz’s description of YHWH’s reversal of social conditions is the OT pattern behind Mary’s Magnificat: “He has brought down rulers from their thrones but has lifted up the humble” (Luke 1:52); both texts frame God’s character as constitutively committed to reversal — the lowly exalted, the mourning raised — a pattern that reaches its ultimate expression in the resurrection of the crucified Son

15.note: “He saves the poor from the cutting words of their enemies and from the grip of the powerful” — the deliverance of the poor and powerless from oppressive speech and exploitation is the content of the jubilee proclamation that Jesus announces at Nazareth: “The Spirit of the Lord is on me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor” (Luke 4:18); YHWH’s character as defender of the poor and silencer of injustice (5:16) is the ground on which Christ’s mission stands

17.note: “See, blessed is the person whom God reproves; do not despise the discipline (mûsar) of the Almighty” — Eliphaz quotes a wisdom tradition about divine discipline; Hebrews 12:5-6 cites the parallel in Proverbs 3:11-12 to interpret Christian suffering: “do not make light of the Lord’s discipline... because the Lord disciplines the one he loves”; the irony is that Eliphaz intends this as an accusation (Job is being disciplined because he sinned), while Hebrews applies it as comfort; the same text functions differently depending on whether the sufferer is genuinely guilty or innocent as Job is

18.note: “For he wounds and then bandages; he strikes down and his own hands heal” — this is the exact logic of Hosea 6:1: “Come, let us return to the LORD. He has torn us to pieces but he will heal us; he has injured us but he will bind up our wounds”; both texts describe a YHWH who is the source of both wound and healing, and both invite a response of trust and return; the pattern reaches its christological apex in 1 Peter 2:24: “by his wounds you have been healed” — the wound that Christ bears is the one that heals

“He wounds and then bandages... his own hands heal” — the divine pattern of wound-and-healing that Eliphaz describes abstractly is enacted concretely in the crucifixion: “He himself bore our sins in his body on the cross, so that we might die to sins and live for righteousness; by his wounds you have been healed” (1 Pet 2:24); 1 Peter 2:24 cites Isaiah 53:5 but the Job 5:18 pattern is the earlier wisdom articulation of the same theological principle — the healer suffers in order to heal

26.note: “You will come to your grave in ripe old age, like a sheaf of grain harvested in its proper season” — the agricultural image of the grain harvested at its proper time is the OT seed of Paul’s resurrection argument in 1 Corinthians 15:37-38: “what you sow does not come to life unless it dies... God gives it a body as he has determined, and to each kind of seed he gives its own body”; the grain harvested at the right season becomes, through the resurrection metaphor, the seed that must die before it comes to life in a transformed body

33.note: There is no arbiter between us who might lay his hand on both of us — Job's longing for a mediator who can bridge the gap between the holy God and the accused human; Paul's 'there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus' is the direct answer to Job's longing: the mediator Job needed exists

Job's explicit longing for a mediator: there is no arbiter between us who might lay his hand on both. He needs someone who can represent him to God and God to him — one who shares both natures and stands in both relationships. Hebrews 9:15: 'Therefore he is the mediator of a new covenant, so that those who are called may receive the promised eternal inheritance, since a death has occurred that redeems them from the transgressions committed under the first covenant.' Christ is the arbiter Job could not find: the one who lays his hand on both — fully God and fully human — and whose death satisfies the covenant's justice while securing the creature's inheritance. Job's longing is among the most explicit anticipations of the incarnation in the OT.

2.note: Job's central anguished question: how can a mortal be in the right before God? — the problem that drives the entire book. This is not rhetorical flourish; Job is asking what ground of righteousness could possibly sustain him in a legal contest with the Almighty. Romans 3:20 answers the question with equal directness: 'by works of the law no human being will be justified in his sight, since through the law comes knowledge of sin.' Job's intuition — that no creature can establish righteousness before God by its own resources — is Paul's foundational anthropological premise. Both Job and Paul arrive at the same cliff's edge; only the gospel provides the bridge Job anticipates in verse 33.

8.note: He alone stretched out the heavens and treads on the waves of the sea — Job's doxological confession of divine power even in the midst of his protest. The one who stretched out the heavens is identified in Colossians 1:16 as the Son: 'all things were created through him and for him.' Job's solitary Creator (he alone) is the undivided divine act that the NT distributes through the trinitarian lens: the Father creates, but the eternal Son is the agent through whom all things — including the stretched-out heavens — were made.

He stretches out the heavens like a curtain — Job 9:8 and Isaiah 40:22 share identical language for the creative act. Isaiah deploys it as comfort for the exiled: the God who stretches out the heavens like a curtain reduces princes to nothing. Job deploys it as awe-filled terror: this is the God with whom he must contend. The same divine majesty that is comfort in Isaiah becomes the problem in Job — which is itself instructive about how the same attribute of God can be experienced differently depending on one's sense of standing before him. The gospel resolves this asymmetry: in Christ the God who stretches out the heavens is Immanuel, God with us.

34.note: If only the mediator would take his rod away, so that his dread would not terrify me — then I could speak without fear. Job's desire is to approach God without the terror that divine holiness produces in fallen creatures. Hebrews 4:16: 'Let us then with confidence draw near to the throne of grace, that we may receive mercy and find grace to help in time of need.' The confidence that Hebrews describes is exactly what Job craves and cannot find: an approach to the divine presence without terror. The Epistle grounds this confidence in the high priest who was tested in every way as we are — the one who himself, in Gethsemane, faced what Job faced and passed through it on behalf of those who could not.

25.note: I know that my Redeemer lives, and at the last he will stand upon the earth — Job's confession of faith in a living Redeemer who will vindicate him after death; the resurrection of Christ is the answer to Job's hope: the Redeemer who lives has stood upon the earth, and because he lives Job will live also

I know that my Redeemer lives, and at the last he will stand upon the earth — Job's confession of the living Redeemer who will vindicate him at the resurrection. John 11:25: 'I am the resurrection and the life. Whoever believes in me, though he die, yet shall he live.' Jesus applies to himself the title that is the content of Job's hope: the one who lives and who makes the dead live. The goʾēl who stands upon the earth at the last is the risen Christ who stands before the tomb of Lazarus and speaks resurrection into existence. Job's desperate, darkness-surrounded hope has a name and a face in the NT.

9.note: He has stripped my honor and taken the crown from my head — Job's description of his social and dignitary stripping as part of his suffering. Philippians 2:7-8: Christ 'emptied himself, by taking the form of a servant, being born in the likeness of men. And being found in human form, he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross.' The kenotic stripping Paul describes is the voluntary form of what happens to Job involuntarily: honor removed, crown taken, the high brought low. Christ's humiliation is the willing acceptance of the condition Job experiences as catastrophic loss — and it becomes the ground of Job's own future restoration.

14.note: My kinsmen have failed me, and my close friends have forgotten me — the abandonment by those closest when suffering intensifies. Matthew 26:56: 'Then all the disciples left him and fled.' The pattern is identical: at the moment of deepest need, the inner circle abandons. Job's isolation is the type of Christ's Gethsemane abandonment — the righteous sufferer deserted by those who should have stood with him. The difference is that Job cannot understand why he is abandoned; Christ enters the abandonment with full knowledge and purpose, so that those who abandon in their weakness might find him present in their own moment of isolation.

My intimate friends have forgotten me; those I loved have turned against me. The total social abandonment — kinsmen, guests, maidservants, wife, brothers, children — is the depth of Job's isolation. Hebrews 13:5: 'I will never leave you nor forsake you.' The promise is the direct antithesis of Job's experience and functions as its answer: the God who appears to have withdrawn in Job's suffering is the one who, in Christ, speaks this promise definitively. The 'never' (ou me + emphatic) is the NT's answer to Job's 'have forgotten me' — an abandonment refused and reversed in the resurrection.

4.note: Where were you when I laid the foundation of the earth? — the divine speech from the whirlwind (chs. 38-41) confronts Job with the Creator's incomprehensible majesty; John 1:1 grounds Christ as the one who was present at that foundation: in the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God — the one speaking from the whirlwind and the one who became flesh are the same person

7.note: When the morning stars sang together, and all the sons of God shouted for joy — the angelic assembly's worship at the laying of creation's foundation; Colossians 1:16 identifies the agent of that founding: by him were all things created, that are in heaven, and that are in earth, visible and invisible, whether they be thrones or dominions or principalities or powers; the 'sons of God' whose joy accompanies creation's birth are themselves created through and for the same preexistent Christ who is the foundation they celebrated

All the sons of God shouted for joy — the angelic multitude's song at creation's founding echoes the innumerable angelic host of Rev 5:11-12 who cry Worthy is the Lamb who was slain, to receive power and wealth and wisdom and might and honour and glory and blessing; the creation-shout of joy and the redemption-shout of worship are the bookend songs of the same angelic chorus, honouring the same Lord at the beginning and the consummation of all things

31.note: Can you bind the chains of the Pleiades or loose the belt of Orion? — God's ironic catechism on astronomical governance; in Revelation's vision of the risen Christ, he had in his right hand seven stars (Rev 1:16), and he identifies himself as he who holds the seven stars (Rev 2:1); the question God directs at Job — can you govern the star-clusters? — has its NT answer in the one who holds all stars in his right hand: the cosmic authority that silences Job is claimed by the risen Lord as his own possession

36.note: Who has put wisdom in the inward parts, or given understanding to the mind? — the rhetorical question assumes that only God can place wisdom within a creature; Jeremiah's new covenant promise answers how this will be done universally: I will put my law in their inward parts and write it on their hearts (Jer 31:33); the inward-placement of wisdom God claims as his prerogative becomes the new covenant's central promise, mediated by the Spirit of Christ (2 Cor 3:3)

Who has put wisdom in the inward parts? — the divine monopoly on inner wisdom that God asserts here is given to believers through the Spirit: for the Spirit searches everything, even the depths of God (1 Cor 2:10); what no creature can access by searching (Job 28:12-13) and no creature can self-install (Job 38:36) is granted as gift — the Spirit as the divine answer to the divine question

4.note: “The Almighty’s arrows are lodged in me; my spirit is drinking their venom; the terrors of God are lined up against me” — Job’s image of divine arrows recalls Psalm 38:2: “your arrows have sunk into me, and your hand has come down on me”; both texts use the arrow image for the affliction that comes directly from God’s hand on the sufferer — not random suffering but aimed suffering; Psalm 38 is David’s lament in illness and abandonment, creating a pattern of royal suffering that points toward the Passion narrative

Job’s “terrors of God are lined up against me” opens the pattern of innocent suffering under God’s perceived assault that Psalm 22 voices most fully: “My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? Why are you so far from saving me?” (Ps 22:1); both Job and Psalm 22 stage the experience of the righteous sufferer who perceives God as hostile rather than absent — the precise experience that Christ enacts on the cross when he cries Ps 22:1 from the darkness of Golgotha

14.note: “The despairing person deserves steadfast love (ḥeseḏ) from a friend; to withhold it is to forsake the fear of the Almighty” — Job identifies ḥeseḏ as what friendship owes to the suffering; John 15:13 defines the ultimate expression of this love: “Greater love has no one than this: to lay down one’s life for one’s friends”; where Job’s friends withhold the covenant loyalty that suffering demands, Christ enacts it in its fullest form — not merely sympathizing with the suffering but entering into death on their behalf

15.note: “My brothers are as treacherous as a seasonal brook, like watercourses that flow away” — Job’s image of the wadi that promises water but vanishes in summer heat is also Jeremiah’s complaint about God himself in Jeremiah 15:18: “Why is my pain unending and my wound grievous and incurable? You are to me like a deceptive brook, like a spring that fails.” Both Job and Jeremiah use the same image — the naḥal ʾākzāḇ (treacherous brook) — to describe the experience of unfulfilled expectation in crisis; the suffering prophet and the suffering patriarch share the same rhetorical vocabulary for abandonment

17.note: The seasonal brookes that “disappear when the heat comes” — the image of water that satisfies temporarily but ultimately fails — is the foil that Jesus’ offer in John 4:14 explicitly addresses: “but whoever drinks the water I give them will never thirst. Indeed, the water I give them will become in them a spring of water welling up to eternal life.” Job’s friends are the human form of the failing brook; Christ is the permanently flowing spring — the ḥeseḏ that Job’s friends withheld, given freely and inexhaustibly

27.note: “You would cast lots (tappîlû ʿal-yāṯôm) over an orphan and sell out your friend” — Job accuses his friends of treating him like an orphan to be disposed of, using the lot-casting image for callous allocation; the lot-casting that reduces the vulnerable person to a commodity appears in its starkest form at the crucifixion: “they divided my clothes among them and cast lots for my garment” (John 19:24 citing Ps 22:18); the reduction of the suffering innocent to a series of property transactions — Job’s estate was already stripped — anticipates the stripping and lotting of Christ

1.note: “Does not man have hard service (ṣāḇāʾ) on earth, and are not his days like those of a hired worker?” — ṣāḇāʾ is the word for military service and the compulsory labour of the covenant people; Job’s description of human life as hard service under a demanding master is the experiential underside of what Christ voluntarily enters: “the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many” (Mark 10:45); the ṣāḇāʾ that Job endures as an unwilling conscript, Christ takes on as a willing servant

7.note: “Remember that my life is but a breath (rûaḥ); my eyes will never again see happiness” — Job’s lament that life is a rûaḥ (breath/wind) is the OT source of James 4:14: “What is your life? You are a mist (atmis) that appears for a little while and then vanishes.” Both texts use the brevity of life as a theological pressure point — in James, as a warning against presumptuous planning; in Job, as a cry of grief; in both, the transience of human life calls for either humility or lament rather than self-sufficient confidence

9.note: “As a cloud vanishes and is gone, so the one who descends to Sheol does not come up” — Job asserts, from within his suffering, the finality of death: no return from Sheol; the “he will never return home” of verse 10 voices the pre-resurrection impossibility that the disciples experienced after the crucifixion; Acts 1:11 is the answer to Job 7:9 — “this same Jesus, who has been taken from you into heaven, will come back in the same way you have seen him go”; the one who descended into death (Acts 2:27 cites Ps 16:10) did not remain in Sheol; the exception that Job cannot imagine becomes the ground of Christian hope

11.note: “I will speak in the anguish of my spirit (bᵉṣar rûḥî) and complain in the bitterness of my soul” — Job’s decision to voice his suffering rather than suppress it is the OT form of what Paul’s pneumatology makes possible for the suffering believer: “the Spirit himself intercedes for us through wordless groans (stenagmois alaletois)” (Rom 8:26); Job’s anguished speech and the Spirit’s inexpressible groaning are on the same theological continuum — both assert that the suffering of the righteous reaches God as genuine prayer, not as faithless complaint

12.note: “Am I the sea, or a sea monster (tannîn), that you place a guard over me?” — Job protests that God treats him as a cosmic enemy requiring surveillance and restraint; the same sea and tannîn (chaos-dragon) imagery appears throughout the Psalms as what YHWH has already defeated (Ps 74:13-14; 89:9-10); Jesus’ rebuking of the sea in Matthew 8:26 (“he rebuked the winds and the waves, and it was completely calm”) demonstrates his authority over precisely the chaotic force Job is accused of being; Christ is not the guard over the sea — he is the one who commands it

17.note: “What is man, that you should make so much of him, and that you fix your attention on him — testing him every morning, examining him every moment?” — Job’s complaint about divine scrutiny inverts Psalm 8:4: “what is mankind that you are mindful of him, human beings that you care for them?” (Ps 8:4); the same question — why does God pay such close attention to human beings? — carries opposite tones: in Psalm 8, wonder and gratitude; in Job 7, dread and exhaustion; the distance between the two uses of the same question maps the distance between creation’s ideal and the fall’s reality

“What is man, that you should make so much of him?” — Hebrews 2:6-9 quotes Psalm 8:4-6 and applies it christologically: “But we do see Jesus, who was made lower than the angels for a little while, now crowned with glory and honor because he suffered death” (Heb 2:9); the question Job voices in anguish — why does God attend so closely to a human being? — receives its ultimate answer in the incarnation: God fixes his attention on humanity because he has become human, taking the full weight of Job’s condition upon himself

21.note: “Why do you not pardon my offense and forgive my sin? For soon I shall lie down in the dust; you will search for me, but I will be gone.” — Job’s plea for forgiveness before death is the OT anticipation of the question the NT answers: why can God not simply pardon? Because the moral weight of sin requires a satisfaction that maintains justice while extending mercy; Matthew 26:28 is the answer: “this is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins” — the pardon Job asks for is made available through the One who lay in the dust (Ps 22:15) and rose, carrying the answer to Job’s plea in his own resurrection

“Why do you not pardon my offense?” — Job’s question assumes that forgiveness is an arbitrary divine choice; Hebrews 9:22 reveals the constraint: “without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness.” The theological answer to Job’s plea is not that God is unwilling but that the mechanism for forgiveness — the priestly sacrifice that provides the blood for atonement — had not yet been provided; what Job begs for from within the old covenant economy is what Hebrews declares available through Christ’s once-for-all offering

3.note: Bildad's foundational premise: Does God pervert justice? Or does the Almighty pervert the right? — the rhetorical question that anchors all three friends' argument. Their logic runs: God is just; Job suffers; therefore Job sinned. Romans 3:5-6 employs the same rhetorical form against a different purpose: 'Is God unjust to inflict wrath? By no means! For then how could God judge the world?' Paul uses the question to establish divine justice as the basis for the gospel's condemnation of sin — including Paul's own. The friends use it to condemn Job. The identical premise yields opposite pastoral conclusions: the friends use divine justice to accuse, Paul uses it to establish mercy for all.

8.note: Bildad's appeal to the ancients: inquire of the former age; consider what the fathers found out. The argument from tradition — the accumulated wisdom of past generations as authoritative — is the wisdom tradition's normal epistemology. Hebrews 1:1-2 invokes precisely this structure before reversing it: 'Long ago, at many times and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers by the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son.' The fathers and their tradition are real and authoritative — but surpassed by the definitive word in the Son. Job's predicament shows the limit of ancestral wisdom: the tradition-consensus says Job sinned; God's verdict says the friends were wrong.

The ancestors investigated and passed down their findings — Bildad assumes their cumulative experience is reliable. 1 Corinthians 10:11 uses ancestral experience in the same way but with a different hermeneutical key: 'these things happened to them as an example, but they were written down for our instruction, on whom the end of the ages has come.' The OT's recorded experiences are typologically significant for NT readers, not simply tradition-proofs. Both uses of ancestral experience acknowledge its theological weight; the NT presses beyond transmission to typological fulfillment.

13.note: The hope of the godless will perish — Bildad's contrast between the one who forgets God (whose hope is cut off, whose trust is a spider's web) and the righteous (who will flourish). 1 Timothy 6:17 identifies the precise error Bildad warns against: 'as for the rich in this present age, charge them not to be haughty, nor to set their hopes on the uncertainty of riches, but on God, who richly provides us with everything to enjoy.' The hope grounded in created wealth and status is spider-web hope: intricate, visible, and ultimately incapable of bearing weight. God alone as the ground of hope is both texts' shared affirmation — though Bildad misapplies it by assuming Job's suffering proves he placed his hope wrongly.

20.note: Bildad's assurance: God will not reject a blameless man — he will yet fill your mouth with laughter. The promise that the blameless man will not be rejected touches the question of the ground on which one stands before God. Philippians 3:9 is Paul's radical restatement of this ground: he does not wish to be found with 'a righteousness of my own that comes from the law, but that which comes through faith in Christ, the righteousness from God that depends on faith.' The blamelessness Bildad promises to the morally upright person is replaced in Christ with the alien righteousness that no suffering can remove, because it rests not on moral achievement but on the completed work of Another.

8.note: Your hands fashioned and made me — and now you turn around and destroy me. Job's protest over the contradiction between God's careful craftsmanship and present apparent abandonment. Psalm 139:14: 'I praise you, for I am fearfully and wonderfully made. Wonderful are your works; my soul knows it very well.' The Psalm turns the same premise — the Creator's intimate involvement in making — toward doxology; Job turns it toward lament. Both are theological responses to the same reality; the difference is not the doctrine but the existential situation. The incarnation takes both responses seriously: Christ himself on the cross cries the abandonment lament while being the one through whom all things were made.

9.note: Remember that you have made me like clay; and will you return me to dust? — Job addressing God as the Potter who now seems to be crushing his own workmanship. Romans 9:20: 'But who are you, O man, to answer back to God? Will what is molded say to its molder, Why have you made me like this?' Paul quotes Isaiah and uses the clay-potter figure to establish divine sovereignty. Job actually speaks the lament that Paul forbids — and God does not rebuke Job for it; the friends who rebuke him are the ones whose speech God judges as wrong (42:7). This distinguishes protest-lament addressed to God (which God hears) from accusation against God's character (which the friends commit by defending God against Job).

12.note: You granted me life and steadfast love, and your care has preserved my spirit — Job's acknowledgment that even his current life is sustained by the God against whom he protests. Acts 17:28: 'In him we live and move and have our being.' Paul quotes a Greek poet to articulate what Job intuits: the creature's very existence is held in the divine sustaining care, not merely at creation but at every moment. Job's protest does not deny this — the preservation of his spirit is what makes his lament possible. The incarnation takes Acts 17:28 to its furthest point: the Son who sustains all things by his word (Heb 1:3) himself entered the created order he sustains.

21.note: Before I go to the place of no return, to the land of darkness and deep shadow — Job's anticipation of Sheol as the horizon of his hopeless condition. John 8:12: 'I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life.' The land of darkness and deep shadow is transformed in the NT not by denying Sheol's reality but by the light of resurrection entering it: Christ went into the realm of death (1 Pet 3:19) and came out the other side, abolishing death's finality and bringing life and immortality to light (2 Tim 1:10). Job's hopeless horizon becomes the very place Christ enters and transforms.

7.note: Can you find out the deep things of God? Can you find out the limit of the Almighty? — Zophar's challenge to Job, intended to humble him but pointing to a genuine truth about divine incomprehensibility. Romans 11:33: 'Oh, the depth of the riches and wisdom and knowledge of God! How unsearchable are his judgments and how inscrutable his ways!' Zophar's rhetorical challenge and Paul's doxological exclamation both point to the same divine hiddenness. The difference: Zophar uses divine incomprehensibility to end Job's protest; Paul arrives at it as the summit of his exposition of the gospel — the mystery of election and mercy that surpasses human accounting. The hiddenness of God is not a conversation-stopper but the mystery into which the gospel reaches.

Zophar asks: can you find out the deep things of God? — the answer assumed to be no, meant to silence Job's questioning. 1 Corinthians 2:10: 'these things God has revealed to us through the Spirit. For the Spirit searches everything, even the depths of God.' Paul's answer to Zophar's question is unexpected: yes — not by human investigation but by divine revelation through the Spirit. The deep things (bathē) of God have been disclosed in Christ and applied by the Spirit. What Zophar meant as a silencing question becomes in the NT the announcement of what has actually been revealed.

17.note: Your life will be brighter than the noonday; its darkness will be like the morning — Zophar's promise of restoration if Job repents. The language of progressive light-increase — darkness becoming morning, morning ascending to noonday — is the idiom of eschatological hope. 2 Peter 1:19: 'We have the prophetic word more fully confirmed...as a lamp shining in a dark place, until the day dawns and the morning star rises in your hearts.' The light-to-noonday arc that Zophar promises conditionally is given unconditionally in Christ: the morning star of his resurrection breaks in the believer's heart and will be consummated in the noonday of the new creation when there is no more night.

9.note: “Who among all these does not know that the hand of the LORD has done this?” — this is the only occurrence of the divine name YHWH in all of Job’s speeches; it emerges in a natural-theology argument: the animals, birds, earth, and fish all testify to God’s sovereign action; Romans 1:20 articulates the same principle: “For since the creation of the world God’s invisible qualities — his eternal power and divine nature — have been clearly seen, being understood from what has been made”; Job’s appeal to creation as witness to the Creator is proto-Pauline natural theology deployed against friends who claim to have a monopoly on divine knowledge

10.note: “In his hand is the life of every living creature and the breath of all human flesh” — Job’s declaration that all life is held in God’s hand is the OT basis for Paul’s Areopagus sermon: “for in him we live and move and have our being” (Acts 17:28); both texts assert absolute creaturely dependence on God for the continuation of life itself — the nepheš (life/breath) of all creatures is not self-sustaining but held as a continuous gift; this is the ground both Job’s suffering and Christ’s incarnation stand on: God holds life, and therefore can both take it and give it back

13.note: “With God are wisdom and strength; he has counsel and understanding” — Job’s description of God as the exclusive possessor of ḥokmāh ûgᵉḇûrāh (wisdom and might) is the background for Paul’s declaration in Colossians 2:3 that in Christ “are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge”; what Job attributes to God in the abstract, the NT locates specifically in the person of Christ — the wisdom that transcends all human counsel is not a divine attribute at a distance but a person who has entered human history

“With God are wisdom and strength” — the pairing of divine wisdom and power that Job describes becomes Paul’s description of Christ in 1 Corinthians 1:24: “Christ the power of God and the wisdom of God” (Christon theou dynamin kai theou sophian); the wisdom that Eliphaz and his friends claim to possess, and that Job insists belongs only to God, is identical with the crucified Christ — “who became for us wisdom from God” (1:30)

17.note: “He leads counselors away stripped of dignity and turns judges into fools” — Job’s doxology of divine sovereignty over human wisdom — stripping advisors, overturning the trusted — is the OT precedent for Paul’s question in 1 Corinthians 1:20: “Where is the wise person? Where is the teacher of the law? Where is the philosopher of this age? Has not God made foolish the wisdom of the world?”; both texts assert the same pattern: divine action renders human wisdom-claims absurd, and the “foolishness” of the gospel (the crucified Christ) is wiser than all human counsel

22.note: “He brings to light the deep things hidden in darkness and brings the shadow of death into the open” — Job’s description of God as the revealer of hidden things anticipates Paul’s eschatological statement in 1 Corinthians 4:5: “he will bring to light what is hidden in darkness and will expose the motives of the heart”; both texts use the same illumination-of-hidden-things pattern for divine disclosure, though Job deploys it about the present world’s hidden governance, while Paul applies it to the judgment that is coming

“He brings to light the deep things hidden in darkness” — Daniel 2:22 uses the same language in a doxology: “He reveals deep and hidden things; he knows what lies in darkness, and light dwells with him”; both Job and Daniel praise God for his access to what is concealed — the ʿᵃmîqāṯāʾ ûmistᵃrāṯāʾ of Dan 2:22 and the nistārôṯ of Job 12:22; in both cases, the hidden things belong to suffering contexts where God’s knowledge of the concealed is the only ground of hope

25.note: “They grope in the dark without light, and he makes them stagger like drunkards” — Job’s description of God removing understanding from rulers so they wander blindly in darkness is the reverse image of John 1:5: “The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it”; the darkness Job describes — powerful people stumbling without divine illumination — is the condition that the incarnation addresses; the world’s leaders groping in darkness is the OT picture of human existence that Christ as phōs tou kosmou (light of the world) enters to address

3.note: “I want to speak directly to the Almighty; I long to argue my case before God” — Job’s longing to approach God directly, bypassing inadequate human mediators, is the OT form of what Hebrews 10:19-22 declares available through Christ: “we have confidence to enter the Most Holy Place by the blood of Jesus... let us draw near to God with a sincere heart and with the full assurance that faith brings”; what Job desperately desires — unmediated access to the divine presence — is exactly what Christ’s high priesthood provides; the barrier that made Job’s direct approach terrifying is the barrier Christ’s death removes

7.note: “Will you speak falsehood on God’s behalf? Will you tell lies for his sake?” — Job accuses his friends of theological deception: they construct a false account of God’s ways to defend God’s honor; John 14:6 presents the contrast — “I am the way and the truth and the life” — in the one who is himself the truth, no advocacy requires distortion; where the friends manufacture theological justifications for God, Christ embodies the truth that makes distortion unnecessary; the friends’ “pious lies” are the negative image of Christ’s truthful mediation

15.note: “Though he kills me, yet I will hope in him” — Job’s declaration of hope against all evidence of God’s hostility is the most direct OT parallel to Habakkuk 3:17-18: “Though the fig tree does not blossom and there are no grapes on the vines...yet I will rejoice in the LORD, I will be joyful in God my Savior”; both texts formulate hope as a defiant act of the will in the face of circumstances that contradict it — hope not grounded in present evidence but in the character of God that transcends current experience

“Though he kills me, yet I will hope in him” — the absolute and unconditional nature of Job’s hope even through death is the OT anticipation of Paul’s climactic declaration in Romans 8:38-39: “I am convinced that neither death nor life... nor anything else in all creation will be able to separate us from the love of God that is in Christ Jesus our Lord”; Job’s hope that not even death can sever his relationship with God is what the resurrection of Christ grounds as certainty rather than mere aspiration

16.note: “This itself will be my deliverance — no godless man can stand in his presence” — Job’s confidence that only the genuinely righteous can stand before God is the OT form of Hebrews 12:14: “without holiness no one will see the Lord”; Job’s claim to be able to stand before God is not arrogance but a declaration of integrity; the NT grounds this confidence not in Job’s own record but in Christ’s righteousness imputed to those who trust him — the ones who “stand before God” in Revelation 7:9 are those who have “washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb”

24.note: “Why do you hide your face from me and treat me as your enemy?” — Job’s experience of God as adversary rather than defender, his face hidden from the innocent sufferer, is the OT pattern that reaches its apex in the cry of dereliction: “My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?” (Matt 27:46 citing Ps 22:1); the divine face-hiding that Job experiences as inexplicable becomes, at the cross, theologically necessary — God turns his face from Christ precisely because Christ has taken on the full weight of human guilt; what Job suffers without explanation, Christ suffers as the explanation

“Why do you hide your face from me?” — the hidden face of God in suffering is answered in Psalm 22:24: “he has not despised or scorned the suffering of the afflicted one; he has not hidden his face from him but has listened to his cry for help”; the same psalm that Jesus cites from the cross (v.1) ends with the assurance that God ultimately does not abandon the righteous sufferer; Job’s present experience of divine hiddenness does not represent the final truth about their relationship

1.note: “Man born of woman is short-lived and full of turmoil. He comes up like a flower and then wilts; he passes like a shadow and does not endure.” — the flower-and-shadow metaphor for human transience is the OT source that Isaiah 40:6-8 develops and that 1 Peter 1:24 quotes directly: “All flesh is like grass, and all its glory like the flowers of the field; the grass withers and the flower falls, but the word of the Lord remains forever”; Job’s grief-saturated meditation on mortality is the experiential context that gives the prophetic and apostolic comfort its weight — the word that endures is the answer to the flesh that fails

4.note: “Who can bring something pure from something impure? No one can.” — Job’s rhetorical question about the impossibility of moral purity from a corrupted source is the OT form of Jesus’ statement in John 3:6: “Flesh gives birth to flesh, but the Spirit gives birth to spirit”; the impossibility Job names — ṭāhôr meṭāmēʾ (pure from impure) — is the same impossibility that makes new birth from above necessary; the conclusion both texts draw is that only divine action can produce what human nature cannot

“Who can bring something pure from something impure? No one can.” — Job’s observation about universal human impurity is the wisdom tradition’s form of Paul’s categorical statement in Romans 3:23: “for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God”; the anthropological pessimism that Job voices from within his suffering becomes the basis for Paul’s universal soteriology — because none are pure, none can save themselves, and the grace that reaches all must come from outside the human condition

7.note: “For there is hope for a tree: if it is cut down, it will sprout again, and its new growth will not stop... yet at the smell of water it will bud and put out branches like a new plant.” — Job uses the cut-down tree that sprouts again as a hopeful image to contrast with human death (vv.10-12), wishing the same were true of man; Jesus fulfills this wish with an exact parallel: “unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit” (John 12:24); Job’s tree-stump hope becomes, through the resurrection, the principle of death-and-new-life that undergirds all Christian hope

“Though its root grows old in the earth and its stump dies in the soil — yet at the smell of water it will bud” — the buried root that gives rise to new growth is Paul’s resurrection analogy in 1 Corinthians 15:37: “what you sow does not come to life unless it dies... You do not plant the body that will be, but just a seed”; Job’s agricultural image of death-and-new-growth anticipates the resurrection logic that Paul applies to the human body — buried as mortal seed, raised as immortal glory

12.note: “So man lies down and does not rise; until the heavens are no more, they will not wake or be roused from their sleep.” — Job’s “until the heavens are no more” is a conditional of hopelessness from within his pre-resurrection perspective; 1 Corinthians 15:20 announces the turn of that condition: “But Christ has indeed been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep”; the “will not rise” of Job 14:12 is answered by the empty tomb — the one who lay down in death became the “firstfruits” whose rising guarantees the general resurrection that Job could only hope for

“They will not wake or be roused from their sleep” — Job’s description of the dead as “asleep” (lōʾ yāqûṣû — they will not be roused) uses the sleep metaphor for death that Paul employs throughout his resurrection teaching; 1 Thessalonians 4:16: “the dead in Christ will rise first” — the “sleep” that Job sees as final, Paul presents as temporary; the waking that Job says will not come “until the heavens are no more” is precisely what Christ’s return accomplishes

13.note: “O that you would hide me in Sheol, shelter me until your anger passes, set a fixed time for me, and then remember me!” — Job’s conditional longing — hide me in death until your wrath is over, then call me out — is the most explicit proto-resurrection hope in the wisdom literature; John 5:28-29 declares its fulfilment: “an hour is coming when all who are in the tombs will hear his voice and come out”; the “calling out” that Job both hopes for and doubts (v.14: “if a man dies, will he live again?”) is announced by Jesus as a certainty — and already enacted in the raising of Lazarus (John 11:43)

14.note: “If a man dies, will he live again? Through all my days of hard service I will wait until my release comes.” — Job’s question is the oldest and most direct form of the resurrection question that Paul addresses in 1 Corinthians 15: “if only for this life we have hope in Christ, we are of all people most to be pitied” (15:19); the hard service Job endures while waiting for his “release” (ḥᵃlîpāh — change/relief) is exactly the condition Paul says faith makes bearable only if resurrection is real; the whole of 1 Corinthians 15 is the answer to Job 14:14

15.note: “You will call out to me, and I will answer; you will long for the work of your hands.” — Job’s hope that God will call and he will answer — that death is not the final word — is the hope Jesus declares himself to be: “I am the resurrection and the life. The one who believes in me will live, even though they die” (John 11:25); the calling-out that Job dreams of, Jesus demonstrates with Lazarus (“Lazarus, come out!”, John 11:43); the longing of God for “the work of his hands” — his creatures — is answered in the incarnation and satisfied in the resurrection

17.note: “My transgression is sealed in a bag, and you have stitched up my guilt.” — Job’s image of sin as a sealed, accumulated record kept against him is the legal metaphor that Colossians 2:14 inverts: “having canceled the charge of our legal indebtedness, which stood against us and condemned us; he has taken it away, nailing it to the cross”; the sealed bag of transgression that Job fears has been formally opened, discharged, and nailed to the instrument of death by the one who took the record of debt upon himself

19.note: “You wash away the hope of man” — Job’s lament reaches its lowest point: even hope is being eroded, worn away like stone by water (vv.18-19); Hebrews 6:19 provides the direct answer: “We have this hope as an anchor for the soul, firm and secure. It enters the inner sanctuary behind the curtain, where our forerunner, Jesus, has entered on our behalf”; the hope that Job’s suffering washes away is the hope that Christ’s entrance into the Holy of Holies anchors permanently — not subject to erosion because it is not grounded in Job’s circumstances but in Christ’s heavenly intercession

14.note: What is man that he can be pure? Or he who is born of a woman, that he can be righteous? — Eliphaz's second-speech argument from human constitution: birth from a woman entails creaturely limitation and moral contamination. John 3:6: 'That which is born of the flesh is flesh, and that which is born of the Spirit is spirit.' Jesus uses the same birth-logic to establish the necessity of new birth: what is generated by flesh produces flesh; only the Spirit's act generates spiritual life. The gospel does not dispute Eliphaz's anthropology — human birth produces beings who cannot of themselves stand righteous before God — but answers it with the new birth that Eliphaz cannot imagine.

16.note: How much less one who is abominable and corrupt, a man who drinks injustice like water! — Eliphaz's escalation from creaturely limitation to active moral depravity. Romans 3:23: 'for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God.' Paul's universal statement and Eliphaz's rhetorical challenge share the same anthropological ground: no human being achieves the righteousness that standing before God requires. The difference is that Paul immediately continues: 'and are justified by his grace as a gift, through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus' (3:24). Eliphaz has the diagnosis; the gospel alone provides the remedy.

20.note: The wicked man writhes in pain all his days — the sound of terror is in his ears, in prosperity the destroyer comes upon him. He does not believe he will return out of darkness, and he is marked for the sword. Hebrews 2:15 names this terror as the universal human condition under the power of death: Christ came to 'deliver all those who through fear of death were subject to lifelong slavery.' Eliphaz intends the terror-filled, darkness-haunted existence as a portrait of the wicked; but Hebrews suggests it describes the human condition in Adam — all are subject to the fear that makes life a form of slavery until liberation comes through Christ's conquest of death.

23.note: He wanders for food — where is it? He knows that a day of darkness is ready at his hand. The description of the wicked man's fearful, food-seeking restlessness — a life without peace — is the shadow of what Christ offers. John 14:27: 'Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you. Not as the world gives do I give to you. Let not your hearts be troubled, neither let them be afraid.' The restless dread Eliphaz assigns to the wicked is the universal condition the gospel addresses: the peace Christ gives does not merely reverse circumstances but transforms the orientation of the heart toward death, darkness, and the future.

18.note: O earth, cover not my blood, and let my cry find no resting place! — Job's appeal to the earth to preserve his blood as witness, refusing to allow his unjust suffering to be silently absorbed. Abel's blood cried out from the ground (Gen 4:10); Job demands the same ongoing witness. Hebrews 12:24 explicitly contrasts this with the blood of Christ: 'Jesus, the mediator of a new covenant, and to the sprinkled blood that speaks a better word than the blood of Abel.' Abel's blood cried for vengeance; Christ's blood speaks forgiveness. Job's blood-cry anticipates both: the demand for vindication that the gospel addresses not by silencing the cry but by the one who absorbed its verdict on behalf of those who deserve it.

19.note: Even now, behold, my witness is in heaven, and he who testifies for me is on high — Job's affirmation that despite all appearances, a heavenly witness exists who holds his case. 1 John 2:1: 'if anyone does sin, we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous.' Job's desperate postulate — a heavenly advocate — is the NT's confident declaration. The heavenly witness Job gropes toward in the darkness of undeserved suffering is identified in the NT as the glorified Christ interceding at the right hand of the Father. Job's faith-assertion against all evidence prefigures the believer's assurance grounded in the completed work of the one who was himself, like Job, accused by the adversary.

21.note: That he would argue the case of a man with God, as a son of man does with his neighbor — Job's longing for a mediator who would plead his case before God with the intimacy a man uses with his friend. Hebrews 5:7: 'In the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications, with loud crying and tears, to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverence.' Christ's Gethsemane prayer is the fulfillment of Job's longing: not a formal legal advocate but one who argues the case with tears, with the intimacy of a son addressing a father, and who was heard — not by being spared suffering but by being raised through it.

The plea on behalf of a man — Job's longing for intercession that will not merely be heard by God but will argue with the force of personal identification with the one suffering. Romans 8:34: 'who is to condemn? Christ Jesus is the one who died — more than that, who was raised — who is at the right hand of God, who indeed is interceding for us.' Paul's intercession is grounded in Christ's completed work: he argues the case not from outside the human condition but from within it — as one who himself suffered unjustly, died, and was vindicated by resurrection. Job's longing is answered not by a neutral arbiter but by one who knows what it is to be in Job's position.

1.note: My spirit is broken; my days are extinct; the graveyard is ready for me. — Job's confession of utter exhaustion: the inner person shattered, life's remaining days numbered, death anticipated. 2 Corinthians 4:16: 'So we do not lose heart. Though our outer self is wasting away, our inner self is being renewed day by day.' Paul's apostolic experience directly parallels Job's description — the outer wasting away, the sense that the graveyard approaches — but Paul writes from the other side of the resurrection: the same suffering that destroys from without becomes the occasion for inner renewal. Job cannot yet see what Paul has been given to see.

15.note: Where then is my hope? Who will see my hope? — Job's desolate question as he anticipates Sheol: if hope goes down with him to the dust, it cannot be real hope. 1 Peter 1:3: 'Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ! According to his great mercy, he has caused us to be born again to a living hope through the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead.' The living hope Peter celebrates is specifically the answer to Job's question: it is hope that does not go down to the dust because it is grounded in a resurrection that goes through the dust and comes out the other side. Job's question is the darkness that precedes the dawn of resurrection hope.

5.note: Indeed, the light of the wicked is put out, and the flame of his fire does not shine — Bildad's portrait of the wicked man's extinction. John 15:6: 'If anyone does not abide in me he is thrown away like a branch and withers; and the branches are gathered, thrown into the fire, and burned.' Both texts use the extinguishing of light and fire as the image of the final condition of those cut off from God. The NT sharpens the typological edge: the light that is extinguished is not merely the lamp of earthly prosperity but the life that comes only from abiding in Christ, who is the light of the world (John 8:12). Without him, the flame goes out.

13.note: It consumes the parts of his skin; the firstborn of death consumes his limbs. The firstborn of death — a personification of death's most destructive power — consumes the wicked man piece by piece. 1 Corinthians 15:26: 'The last enemy to be destroyed is death.' Paul's eschatological language takes Bildad's poetic figure and places it in the horizon of Christ's victory: death as the ultimate enemy, the firstborn of which Bildad speaks, has been defeated in principle in the resurrection and will be destroyed finally at the last day. What Bildad presents as the wicked man's fate becomes in the NT the enemy Christ absorbs and conquers — entering death's domain (the firstborn of death's territory) and emerging as the firstborn from the dead (Col 1:18).

The firstborn of death devours — the personification of death as an active destroyer, consuming the wicked. Revelation 20:14: 'Then Death and Hades were thrown into the lake of fire. This is the second death, the lake of fire.' The firstborn of death that Bildad uses as a figure for extreme destruction is itself destroyed in the Revelation's final judgment: Death personified is thrown into the lake of fire. The creature that Bildad's poetics makes fearsome is undone by the one who holds the keys of death and Hades (Rev 1:18) — the risen Christ who conquered the firstborn of death by passing through death and returning.

18.note: He is thrust from light into darkness, and driven out of the world — Bildad's picture of the wicked man's final expulsion from light and life into darkness. John 3:19: 'And this is the judgment: the light has come into the world, and people loved the darkness rather than the light because their works were evil.' Both texts use the movement from light to darkness as the structure of judgment. Bildad describes the wicked man's end; John describes the mechanism of self-judgment: the light came into the world in Christ, and those who refused it chose darkness. The thrust from light that Bildad presents as an external punishment is revealed in John as the logical consequence of a prior choice.

5.note: The exulting of the wicked is short, and the joy of the godless but for a moment — Zophar's principle that the prosperity of the unrighteous is temporary. 1 Peter 5:4: 'And when the chief Shepherd appears, you will receive the unfading crown of glory.' The contrast between the brief triumph of the wicked and the unfading crown awaiting the faithful is the NT's eschatological expansion of Zophar's principle. Zophar intends it as a retributive mechanism within history; the NT locates the definitive triumph not in historical reversal but in the appearing of the chief Shepherd — the moment when all temporary exaltations are measured against the permanent.

The triumph of the wicked is short, the moment of the godless brief — Zophar states a principle that the Revelation enacts dramatically. Revelation 20:10: 'the devil who had deceived them was thrown into the lake of fire and sulfur...and they will be tormented day and night forever and ever.' The brevity of the wicked's triumph is the hinge between the present age and the age to come: what appears permanent and triumphant — the serpent's dominion — is revealed as terminally brief from the perspective of the final judgment. Zophar's principle is correct; only the mechanism is different from what he imagines.

15.note: He swallows down riches and vomits them up again; God casts them out of his belly — the image of wealth that cannot be retained, that reverses course at the moment of expected consolidation. Luke 12:20: 'But God said to him, Fool! This night your soul is required of you, and the things you have prepared, whose will they be?' The rich fool's accumulated grain and the wicked man's swallowed riches share the same structural irony: wealth amassed at the moment of supposed triumph is precisely when it is taken. Both texts use this to establish that wealth accumulated apart from God does not constitute security but the illusion of it.

He swallows riches — the image of consuming wealth as an end in itself, only to lose it violently. 1 Timothy 6:9: 'those who desire to be rich fall into temptation, into a snare, into many senseless and harmful desires that plunge people into ruin and destruction.' The vomiting-up of swallowed riches in Job 20 is the dramatic form of what Paul describes as the dynamic of greed: the wealth that is consumed as an ultimate good produces a kind of destruction that is internal to the act of consuming it. Both texts use physical imagery (swallowing, plunging) to convey the self-destructive nature of wealth sought as ultimate security.

7.note: Why do the wicked live on, reach old age, and grow mighty in power? — Job's direct challenge to Zophar's neat retribution theology. This is exactly the question of Psalm 73:3: 'I was envious of the arrogant when I saw the prosperity of the wicked.' The Psalmist's near-loss of faith and Job's counter-argument both refuse to pretend the observed world confirms simple retribution. Both are honest about the scandal of wicked prosperity. The Psalm resolves the tension eschatologically (73:17: entering the sanctuary, understanding their end); the NT grounds that eschatology in the resurrection judgment of Christ (Acts 17:31).

Why do the wicked prosper and grow old? — the question that appears to implicate divine indifference or injustice. 2 Peter 3:9: 'The Lord is not slow to fulfill his promise as some count slowness, but is patient toward you, not wishing that any should perish, but that all should reach repentance.' Peter addresses the same observable delay — the wicked prospering while judgment seems absent — and reinterprets the delay not as divine indifference but as mercy extended to make repentance possible. What looks like the wicked prospering indefinitely is the time of grace; the judgment that will come is certain, and the reason it has not yet come is love, not weakness.

16.note: Behold, is not their prosperity in their own hand? The counsel of the wicked is far from me. — Job's observation that the wicked acknowledge no dependence on God for their success. Luke 12:20 is the Lucan commentary: the rich fool also treats his prosperity as in his own hand — 'I will say to my soul, Soul, you have ample goods laid up for many years; relax, eat, drink, be merry' — until the night when his soul is required. The prosperity that appears to be in one's own hand is revealed to be held at the pleasure of the one who grants the breath that makes prosperity possible.

31.note: Who will declare his way to his face? Who will repay him for what he has done? — Job's question about accountability: who can confront the prosperous wicked man with the truth of his ways? John 16:8: 'And when he comes, he will convict the world concerning sin and righteousness and judgment.' The Spirit sent by Christ is the answer to Job's question: the one who will declare to the wicked man his way is the Paraclete, the Spirit of truth who convicts precisely where human accusation fails or falls silent. What no person in Job's world could accomplish — a truthful accounting before the powerful — the Spirit of the risen Christ accomplishes through the proclamation of the gospel.

Who will repay him for what he has done? — the accountability question Job poses for the apparently untouchable wicked. Acts 17:31: 'because he has fixed a day on which he will judge the world in righteousness by a man whom he has appointed; and of this he has given assurance to all by raising him from the dead.' The judge who will repay has been appointed and authenticated by the resurrection. Job's unanswered question — who will confront the wicked with their deeds? — finds its answer not in history's moral arc but in the fixed day of judgment that the resurrection secures. The risen Christ is both the assurance of judgment and, for those who receive him, the answer to judgment.

6.note: Eliphaz levels a series of social-sin accusations against Job: seizing collateral unjustly, withholding water from the weary traveler, withholding bread from the starving, exploiting the powerful’s land-grab, turning widows away empty-handed, breaking the orphan’s strength (vv.6-9) — none of which Job has actually done, as the reader knows from chapters 1-2 and Job’s own testimony in chapter 31; the list of sins Eliphaz invents maps exactly onto the corporal works of mercy that Jesus commends in Matthew 25:42-43: “I was hungry and you gave me no food... thirsty and you gave me nothing to drink... naked and you did not clothe me”; Eliphaz’s false charges describe the sins of omission that Matthew 25 condemns — which makes Job a type of the one who was unjustly accused of violating covenant obligations he had in fact fulfilled

12.note: “Is not God in the heights of heaven? See how high the stars tower above you.” — Eliphaz invokes divine transcendence as evidence of Job’s culpability; Isaiah 57:15 holds the same truth in tension with its opposite: “For this is what the high and exalted One says — he who lives forever, whose name is holy: ‘I live in a high and holy place, but also with the one who is contrite and lowly in spirit’”; divine height does not entail divine distance from the lowly; the incarnation is the definitive answer to Eliphaz’s implicit deism — the transcendent God descends precisely to the sufferer Eliphaz dismisses

21.note: “Make peace with him now and be reconciled; in that way good will find you.” — Eliphaz’s counsel to Job is theologically correct even though his diagnosis of Job’s condition is wrong; the NT articulation of the same call comes from Paul in 2 Corinthians 5:20: “We implore you on behalf of Christ, be reconciled to God”; the difference is that Eliphaz grounds reconciliation in Job’s moral reform (“banish iniquity from your household”, v.23), while Paul grounds it in the atonement of Christ — “God made him who had no sin to be sin for us, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God” (5:21)

26.note: “Then you will take your delight in the Almighty, and lift your face toward God.” — Eliphaz’s promise of restored delight in God echoes Psalm 37:4: “take delight in the LORD, and he will give you the desires of your heart”; both texts present ʿōneg (delight/pleasure in God) as the experiential outcome of right relationship; the NT grounds this delight not in moral achievement but in union with Christ — “rejoice in the Lord always” (Phil 4:4) is the NT form of the same call, now available through the reconciliation Eliphaz only hints at

27.note: “You will pray to him and he will answer; you will fulfill what you have vowed.” — Eliphaz’s conditional promise (if you return, your prayers will be answered) is given by Jesus as an unconditional gift to those abiding in him: “If you abide in me, and my words abide in you, ask whatever you wish, and it will be done for you” (John 15:7); the condition that Eliphaz imposes — moral reform — Christ replaces with union — “abide in me”; the prayer-answer relationship Eliphaz holds out as a goal is what Jesus gives as a present reality to those in him

3.note: “If only I knew where to find him — if only I could reach the place where he dwells!” — Job’s longing to locate God and present his case is the OT form of the universal human desire for access to the divine presence; Jesus answers Job’s “if only” with a declaration: “I will come again and will take you to myself, that where I am you may be also” (John 14:3); and moments later: “I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me” (John 14:6) — the “place where God dwells” that Job cannot find is opened by the one who is himself the way

“If only I could reach the place where he dwells!” — the inaccessibility of God that Job laments is the same barrier that Hebrews 4:16 declares removed: “let us then with confidence draw near to the throne of grace, that we may receive mercy and find grace to help in time of need”; the confidence to approach God that Job desperately desires but cannot achieve is exactly the gift that Christ’s high priesthood provides — the barrier is down; the way is open

10.note: “But he knows the path I walk; when he has tried me, I will come out as gold.” — Job’s confidence that divine testing will ultimately refine rather than destroy him is the OT source of 1 Peter 1:7: “these have come so that the proven genuineness of your faith — of greater worth than gold, which perishes even though refined by fire — may result in praise, glory and honor when Jesus Christ is revealed”; both texts use the metallurgical image of gold-refining for suffering that tests and purifies; Job’s hope that his tested faith will emerge as gold becomes the NT’s framework for interpreting Christian suffering

12.note: “I have valued the words of his mouth more than my daily bread (miḥuqqî).” — Job’s declaration that divine speech exceeds bread in value is the OT precedent for Jesus’ response to the devil in Matthew 4:4, quoting Deuteronomy 8:3: “man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word that comes from the mouth of God”; both texts assert the same priority: the nourishment provided by God’s word surpasses the nourishment of physical food; Job voices this conviction from within his suffering — without food, without health, he still values divine speech above the bodily sustenance he has lost

13.note: “But he is resolute in his purpose (bᵉʾeḥāḏ ûmî yᵉšîḇennû), and who can change him? What he wills, that he carries out.” — Job’s acknowledgment of divine immutability of purpose, though voiced in dread, articulates what Hebrews 6:17 celebrates: “God, desiring even more to show to the heirs of the promise the unchangeable nature of his purpose, intervened with an oath”; the same divine resoluteness that fills Job with dread (v.15) is the ground of the Christian’s hope — the immutable God who cannot change his purpose has bound himself by oath to the heirs of salvation

7.note: “They spend the night naked, with no garment to cover them against the cold.” — Job’s survey of the exploited poor (chapter 24) catalogs the exact conditions that Jesus identifies with himself in the final judgment: “I was naked and you clothed me” (Matt 25:36); the naked poor who shelter against rocks (v.8), the hungry who carry others’ harvest while starving (v.10), the fatherless snatched from their mothers (v.9) — Job’s theodicy complaint about God’s apparent indifference to this suffering is answered in Christ’s identification with “the least of these” (Matt 25:40)

12.note: “From the city the dying groan; the mortally wounded cry out — yet God lays no charge of wrongdoing.” — Job’s theodicy complaint that God seems silent in the face of urban suffering and violent death anticipates the cry of the martyrs under the altar in Revelation 6:10: “How long, O Lord, holy and true, until you judge the inhabitants of the earth and avenge our blood?”; both texts voice the same experience — the suffering righteous cry out and the divine judgment seems delayed; the answer given in Revelation (a little longer) and ultimately in Christ’s return is the answer to Job’s complaint

13.note: “These are among those who rebel against the light; they do not know its ways and will not walk its paths.” — Job’s description of the wicked as constitutive rebels against light is the OT form of John 3:19-20: “this is the judgment: the light has come into the world, and people loved the darkness rather than the light because their works were evil. For everyone who does wicked things hates the light and does not come to the light, lest his works should be exposed”; the moral aversion to light that Job describes as a behavioral pattern becomes, in the incarnation, the response to a person — the Light of the World who is rejected by those who prefer darkness

4.note: “How can any mortal be declared righteous (yiṣdaq) before God? How can anyone born of a woman be clean?” — Bildad’s rhetorical question, the third and final speech of the Friends, states the conclusion all three have been driving toward: human beings are structurally incapable of meeting divine standards; Romans 3:20 is Paul’s NT statement of the identical principle: “no human being will be justified in his sight by works of the law”; both texts presuppose that the question they raise has no human answer — which is why the NT’s answer is the righteousness that comes through faith in Christ (Rom 3:21-22)

“How can anyone born of a woman be clean?” — Bildad’s question identifies the problem precisely: birth from a woman means participation in human impurity; Galatians 4:4 announces the startling solution: “God sent forth his Son, born of woman, born under the law, to redeem those who were under the law”; the incarnation does not bypass the condition Bildad describes — it enters it fully; Christ is born of woman (under the very condition that disqualifies), in order to provide the cleansing that birth from woman cannot generate

6.note: “How much less a mortal, who is a maggot, and a human being, who is a worm!” — Bildad’s climactic deprecation of humanity uses the worm (tôlāʿāh) image that Psalm 22:6 places on the lips of the righteous sufferer: “I am a worm and not a man, scorned by everyone, despised by the people”; the Psalm’s worm is Christ’s own self-identification in Gethsemane-and-cross — the Son of God taking the lowest human valuation upon himself; Bildad uses the worm to crush Job’s dignity; Psalm 22 uses it to identify the Messiah’s humiliation; Christ fulfills both by becoming the worm-man who rises as the firstborn from the dead

“A human being, who is a worm” — Bildad’s contemptuous diminishment of humanity is what Christ voluntarily enters; Philippians 2:7 describes the kenosis: “he made himself nothing by taking the very nature of a servant, being made in human likeness”; the one who is equal with God takes on the condition that Bildad describes as maximally contemptible — mortality, lowliness, dependence — not to confirm the contempt but to redeem the condition from within

“Sheol is laid bare before him, and Abaddon has no covering.” — Job’s doxology asserts that even the realm of the dead is transparent to God — no darkness shields it; Revelation 1:18 presents the christological fulfilment: “I am the living one. I died, and behold I am alive forevermore, and I have the keys of Death and Hades.” The transparency of Sheol before God becomes, in the resurrection, the authority of Christ over Sheol — not merely that he can see it, but that he entered it and holds its keys

“Abaddon (ʾᵃḇaddôn) has no covering” — Job’s use of ʾᵃḇaddôn (destruction/the abyss) as a name for the underworld is the OT source of Revelation 9:11: “They have as king over them the angel of the bottomless pit. His name in Hebrew is Abaddon”; the realm that Job says is bare before God becomes in Revelation the domain of a defeated adversary whose king has been overthrown by the Lamb (Rev 5:5-6)

7.note: “He stretches out the north over the void and hangs the earth on nothing (ʿal-bᵉlî-māh).” — Job’s cosmological doxology — the earth suspended over emptiness by divine power alone — is the OT expression of what Hebrews 1:3 attributes to Christ: “upholding the universe by the word of his power”; the hanging of the earth on nothing that Job celebrates as a work of the Creator is the continuous act of the Son through whom “all things hold together” (Col 1:17); the doxology Job directs to YHWH is the doxology the NT directs to Christ

11.note: “The pillars of heaven tremble and are stunned at his rebuke.” — the trembling of cosmic structures before divine rebuke anticipates the eschatological shaking of Hebrews 12:26: “yet once more I will shake not only the earth but also the heavens” (citing Hag 2:6); both texts present the trembling of the created order as the appropriate response to divine majesty; in Hebrews, this cosmic shaking is connected to the “kingdom that cannot be shaken” — the unshakeable realm that the shaken heavens and earth reveal by contrast

12.note: “By his power he calmed the sea (yārag hayyām); by his understanding he crushed the sea dragon.” — Job’s doxology of God as the one who stills the chaotic sea is the OT basis for recognizing Christ’s authority when he rebukes the Sea of Galilee: “Peace! Be still!” (Mark 4:39); the calming that Job attributes to YHWH, Jesus enacts — implicitly claiming the same authority; the disciples’ response (“Who then is this, that even the wind and the sea obey him?” Mark 4:41) is the question that Job’s doxology has already answered: it is YHWH

“By his understanding he crushed the sea dragon (tannîn).” — the defeated sea-dragon of cosmogonic conflict appears in Job 26:12 as the one whom YHWH crushed at creation; Colossians 2:15 presents the definitive historical enactment of the same victory: “having disarmed the rulers and authorities, he put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him [the cross]”; the crushing of the tannîn that Job celebrates as a cosmic deed is accomplished through the cross and resurrection — the dragon’s defeat is finalized in Christ’s triumph
